name: prefer-explicit-naming
content: Prefer explicit naming over implicit naming. Use descriptive names that reveal the intent of the code and accurately reflect all responsibilities of the component or function (e.g., prefer 'CategoriesDeadStockBarChart' over 'DeadStockByCategoryBarChart', 'CategoriesLowStock' over 'LowStockByCategories'). Avoid redundant or overly verbose prefixes, abbreviations, and names that mislead about behavior or omit important context — and ensure filenames are renamed consistently alongside component renames.

name: prefer-functional-react-component-over-classes
content: Prefer functional components over class components. Use functional components with hooks instead of class components.

name: always-add-unit-tests-on-new-api-route
content: Always add unit tests on new API routes. Use the @nestjs/testing package to create a test module and test the route.

name: avoid-unexplained-async-timing-constructs
content: Avoid using async timing constructs like setImmediate, setTimeout, or setInterval unless their purpose is clearly documented. If such constructs are necessary, add a comment explaining why they are needed; otherwise, prefer colocating loading or async state handling inside the component directly responsible for it (e.g., manage loading state inside a data-fetching child component rather than simulating it with a timeout in a parent).

name: add-aria-labels-for-accessibility
content: Add aria-label attributes to interactive and meaningful UI elements to ensure accessibility. This is especially important for buttons, inputs, icons, and custom components that may not have visible text labels.

name: wrap-async-functions-in-usecallback
content: Wrap async functions defined inside React components with useCallback to improve clarity, avoid unnecessary re-renders, and make future migration to server components easier.

name: use-memo-for-derived-state
content: Use useMemo to compute derived or filtered data from state or props instead of recalculating inline on every render. This improves performance and makes the data flow more explicit.

name: use-explicit-typescript-types
content: Use explicit TypeScript types and type assertions to prevent type errors, especially when type changes in one place can propagate and break other parts of the code. Prefer typed assertions (e.g., `[] as MyType[]`) over untyped fallbacks, and always annotate function parameters with their precise types (e.g., `file: File | null` instead of leaving them implicit or typed as `any`).

name: use-auto-loading-mechanisms
content: Prefer auto-loading mechanisms (e.g., @fastify/autoload for routes) over manually registering each module individually. This reduces boilerplate and keeps the codebase easier to maintain as it grows.

name: avoid-implicit-type-coercion
content: Avoid using implicit type coercion operators (e.g., unary `+` to convert a value to a number) without a clear explanation. If type conversion is necessary, use explicit methods like `Number()`, `parseInt()`, or `parseFloat()` and add a comment if the intent is not obvious.

name: ensure-lint-passes-before-merging
content: Ensure all lint checks pass before merging a PR. Add any required ESLint plugins or packages to the configuration so the pipeline does not fail.

name: prefer-layout-component-props-over-inline-styles
content: Prefer passing layout-related props (e.g., gap, maxHeight, overflowY, marginTop) directly to layout components (e.g., Stack, HStack) rather than using inline style objects or utility CSS workarounds. This keeps styling consistent with the component system's API.

name: avoid-non-null-assertions-on-optional-props
content: Avoid passing potentially undefined or optional values to component props that require a defined value. Ensure required props are always defined before passing them, using conditional rendering or fallbacks to handle undefined cases safely.

name: track-pagination-and-data-limits
content: When fetching lists of data, ensure that pagination or infinite loading is implemented to avoid silently returning only the first page of results. If not implemented immediately, create a follow-up issue to track the work.

name: remove-debug-statements-before-merging
content: Remove all debug statements such as console.log, console.warn, and console.error, as well as unnecessary try/catch blocks that only log errors, before merging a PR. Debug output and empty error handlers should not appear in production code.

name: prefer-existing-loading-state-over-local-state
content: Prefer using loading or status variables already provided by hooks (e.g., mutation or query loading states from useQuery or useMutation) instead of introducing redundant local state variables. This reduces state complexity and keeps the component logic consistent with the data-fetching layer.

name: use-framework-native-navigation-components
content: Use the navigation components or APIs provided by the framework (e.g., Next.js Link component or useRouter) instead of programmatic alternatives like `window.location.href` for navigation or plain HTML elements like `<a>` tags for internal navigation. This ensures correct client-side routing behavior, better performance, and framework-level optimizations.

name: prefer-conditional-rendering-over-ternary-chains
content: When rendering multiple mutually exclusive UI states (e.g., multi-step flows), prefer separate conditional blocks (e.g., multiple `if` or `{condition && ...}` expressions) over deeply nested ternary chains. This improves readability and makes it easier to add or remove steps.

name: implement-multi-step-flows-with-navigation
content: When implementing multi-step UI flows (e.g., registration wizards), ensure users can navigate both forward and backward between steps. Include a 'previous step' mechanism to avoid trapping users in a linear, unidirectional flow.

name: colocate-components-in-relevant-modules
content: Place new components, modals, and utilities inside the module or feature folder they logically belong to (e.g., a granularity selector used across insights should live in an insights module rather than a stock or common module). Avoid scattering feature-specific code in unrelated or generic directories, and extract reused components into shared modules when they are used across multiple pages.

name: keep-business-logic-at-appropriate-level
content: Place business logic and data transformations at the highest component level where they are needed, rather than pushing them into lower-level child components. Child components should remain focused on their primary responsibility and receive pre-computed values via props.

name: avoid-unnecessary-optional-chaining
content: Avoid using optional chaining (`?.`) on values that are guaranteed to be defined at the point of use. If a value can genuinely be undefined, reflect that in the component's prop types and handle the undefined case explicitly (e.g., via conditional rendering or a fallback) rather than masking it with optional chaining on an always-defined prop.

name: prefer-dangerous-set-inner-html-prop
content: When rendering raw HTML content in React, use the built-in `dangerouslySetInnerHTML` prop on a standard HTML element (e.g., `<div>`) rather than wrapping it in a custom component unnecessarily. If reuse is needed across the codebase, promote the component to a shared UI library.

name: promote-reusable-components-to-shared-library
content: Generic, reusable UI components (e.g., Tag variants, HtmlRenderer, Callout, ItemTag, EllipsedText, formatters, generic wrappers) should be placed in the shared component library (e.g., legos) as variants or new primitives, ideally with a Storybook story, rather than imported from feature-specific modules or duplicated across feature modules. This ensures consistency and makes the component available to all consumers.

name: colocate-styles-with-components
content: Place component-specific CSS rules in the component's own CSS module or scoped stylesheet rather than in global stylesheets. Global CSS files should only contain truly global styles such as resets, tokens, or base layers.

name: keep-components-stateless-via-callback-props
content: Keep reusable UI components stateless by lifting side effects (e.g., opening modals, triggering mutations) out of the component and exposing them as callback props (e.g., onEdit, onDelete, onResolve). When a button or action component is specific enough to own its own mutation (e.g., CaptureOrderPaymentButton managing its own useCapturePayment call), prefer self-contained components over passing callbacks from a parent. This makes components easier to test, reuse, and reason about.

name: avoid-dedicated-css-modules-for-trivial-styles
content: Avoid creating a dedicated CSS module file for a single trivial style rule (e.g., one width declaration). Use inline styles or a component system's built-in props instead, and only introduce a CSS module when there are multiple or complex styles to manage.

name: scope-cache-invalidation-to-affected-queries
content: When invalidating or refetching data after a mutation, target only the specific query or queries that are affected rather than broadly refetching all queries. This avoids unnecessary network requests and keeps data-fetching efficient.

name: use-nullish-coalescing-for-safe-array-defaults
content: When accessing potentially null or undefined nested data (e.g., from API responses), use nullish coalescing (`?? []`) to provide a safe default array before chaining array methods like `.map()`. This prevents runtime errors and makes the intent explicit.

name: avoid-deprecated-dependencies
content: Avoid introducing or using deprecated dependencies in the codebase. Replace deprecated packages with their recommended alternatives (e.g., prefer Panda CSS over Emotion for styling) and ensure all new code uses up-to-date, supported libraries.

name: use-lookup-maps-for-conditional-rendering
content: When conditionally rendering different components or elements based on a string key or type (e.g., payment brand, icon type), prefer a lookup map (e.g., `const map = { key: <Component /> }`) over if/else chains or switch statements. This improves readability and makes it easy to extend with new cases.

name: extract-reusable-logic-into-custom-hooks
content: When a component contains non-trivial stateful or side-effectful logic that could be reused elsewhere, extract it into a custom hook. This improves separation of concerns, testability, and reusability across the codebase.

name: remove-stale-todo-comments-or-resolve-them
content: Remove or resolve stale TODO/FIXME comments, leftover code comments (e.g., commented-out code such as `// overflow="hidden"`, progress markers, notes no longer relevant), and unused components or files before merging a PR. If the work is still pending, ensure the comment is tracked via a proper issue rather than left in the code.

name: prefer-framework-ui-primitives-over-custom-implementations
content: Before implementing custom UI behavior, components, or styling (e.g., custom tooltips, separators, dialog logic), check whether the component library or framework already provides a built-in primitive or pattern (e.g., a `Tooltip`, `Separator`, or `Stack` component). Leveraging existing primitives reduces complexity, avoids duplication, and ensures consistent behavior across the application.

name: create-dedicated-components-for-distinct-ui-flows
content: When a UI action or flow has its own distinct logic, loading state, and title (e.g., CaptureOrderPayment, DeliverOrderButton, AddToCartButton, DeleteInvitationDialog, CopyToClipboardButton), extract it into a dedicated component rather than inlining all logic in a parent page or container. This keeps each component focused on a single responsibility and makes it easier to reuse or modify independently.

name: prefer-query-options-over-useeffect-for-polling
content: When implementing polling behavior (e.g., with Apollo's `pollInterval`), prefer configuring it directly via query options (e.g., `pollInterval: data.length ? 3000 : 0`) rather than managing it imperatively with `useEffect`. This reduces side-effect complexity and keeps data-fetching logic declarative.

name: declare-static-functions-outside-components
content: Declare pure or stateless helper functions, objects, and constants (e.g., formatters, converters, icon/style mappers, default objects, event handlers that do not close over component state) outside the React component body rather than inside it or inline within JSX. Values defined inside a component are recreated on every render; moving them outside improves rendering performance and makes their independence from component state explicit.

name: use-canonical-package-import-paths
content: Import from the canonical, top-level package path (e.g., `@legos/icons` for icons, `@legos/primitives` for UI primitives) rather than deep internal or implementation-specific sub-paths (e.g., `@radix-ui/react-icons`). This keeps imports accurate, avoids coupling to internal package structure, and respects module boundaries.

name: use-enum-for-magic-string-constants
content: Replace magic string literals used for status values, types, or other categorizations with enums or named constants. This improves type safety, discoverability, and makes the codebase easier to refactor when values change.

name: use-shared-modals-to-avoid-promise-based-callbacks
content: When a destructive or asynchronous action (e.g., delete) is triggered from a menu or action component, prefer opening a shared confirmation modal rather than passing a Promise-based callback (e.g., `onDelete: () => Promise<void>`). This simplifies the component's API, avoids async complexity in the caller, and ensures a consistent confirmation UX across the app.

name: centralize-shared-styles-in-design-system
content: Centralize shared interaction styles (e.g., focus rings, hover states) in the shared component library or design system primitives rather than duplicating them per component. This ensures a consistent look and feel across the entire application.

name: extract-shared-utility-functions
content: When the same utility function or logic appears in multiple places within a codebase, extract it into a shared module or utility file rather than duplicating it. Reusing a single shared function reduces maintenance burden and ensures consistent behavior across the codebase.

name: avoid-css-important-overrides
content: Avoid using `!important` in CSS or CSS-in-JS declarations unless absolutely necessary, as it indicates a specificity conflict that should be resolved at the component or design system level instead. If `!important` seems required, investigate whether the underlying component can be fixed or extended to support the needed style via props.

name: prefer-design-system-token-styling
content: When styling components, prefer using design system tokens (e.g., spacing variables from tokens.css, color tokens) and component-level props over raw CSS values, hardcoded hex colors, or framework utility overrides. This ensures consistency with the design system's conventions, keeps styles maintainable, and aligns code with the design source of truth (e.g., Figma tokens).

name: extract-magic-values-to-named-variables
content: When a literal value (e.g., a string, number, or CSS value such as `borderRadius="6px"`) is used directly on a component or duplicated across components, extract it into a named constant, design system token, or expose it as a configurable prop. This improves readability, makes future updates easier to apply consistently, and avoids silent divergence between similar components.

name: propagate-style-fixes-to-design-system
content: When a style change (e.g., border, spacing, shadow, or layout behavior) should apply consistently across all instances of a shared component, make the change in the design system or shared component library rather than overriding it at the feature level. Before adding a style property to a shared component (e.g., boxShadow on a Tooltip), verify the intent is to apply it universally across the entire platform. This ensures the fix is reflected everywhere the component is used.

name: minimize-graphql-query-fields-to-cache-needs
content: When writing GraphQL mutations or queries, only select the fields actually needed for cache updates or UI display rather than over-fetching. If a mutation only changes a specific field (e.g., status), query only that field and the identifier so Apollo Client can automatically update the cache without unnecessary data transfer.

name: remove-unused-imports
content: Remove unused imports before merging a PR. Unused imports add noise, can confuse readers about dependencies, and may cause lint warnings or errors.

name: use-current-color-for-svg-icons
content: When adding SVG icons to the codebase, use `currentColor` for fill or stroke values instead of hardcoded color values. This allows icons to inherit the color of their parent context and makes them easier to theme and reuse.

name: avoid-unused-callback-props
content: Only expose callback props (e.g., onEdit, onDelete) on a component when they are necessary for the component's use cases. If a callback prop is only relevant for a specific, non-general context, question whether it belongs in the component's API or should be handled by the caller instead.

name: use-lowercase-string-literals-for-action-values
content: Use lowercase string literals for action or event values passed through callbacks and props (e.g., 'deliver' | 'cancel' | 'archive' instead of 'Deliver' | 'Cancel' | 'Archive'). This follows conventional naming for programmatic string identifiers and avoids confusion with display labels.

name: prefer-component-props-over-css-overrides
content: Before adding custom CSS classes, inline styles, or creating a className (e.g., using `css({})` for layout or typography values like `position`, `flexShrink`, `gap`, color), check whether the component already supports the desired behavior via built-in props. Prefer using component props over custom CSS overrides or inline style objects to stay consistent with the design system's API.

name: display-formatted-values-in-ui
content: Always display user-facing values (e.g., prices, dates, quantities) in their formatted form rather than raw data. Apply the appropriate formatter (e.g., currency formatter for prices) before rendering values to ensure a consistent and readable UI.

name: hide-unavailable-actions-instead-of-disabling
content: Avoid displaying actions or buttons that are permanently disabled or irrelevant in the current context. Instead, conditionally hide them until they become valid or applicable. Showing disabled actions by default adds visual noise and can confuse users about what is available.

name: match-icons-to-actions
content: Ensure icons used in UI elements (e.g., menu items, buttons) accurately represent the action they trigger. Mismatched icons (e.g., using an edit/pencil icon for a non-edit action) can mislead users; choose an icon that clearly conveys the intended operation.

name: maintain-consistent-page-layout-patterns
content: Keep the layout and structure of similar pages consistent across the application (e.g., stat cards always appear at the top, filters in the same position). If a new page deviates from the established pattern, verify the deviation is intentional and aligned with the design before merging.

name: avoid-duplicate-components-with-minor-variations
content: Avoid creating near-duplicate components that differ only in minor details like text content or color. Instead, generalize the component to accept those values as props, enabling reuse across different pages or modules.

name: never-expose-server-error-messages-to-users
content: Do not display raw server or API error messages directly to end users. Instead, show user-friendly, localized error messages and log the technical details for debugging purposes. Exposing server error messages can leak implementation details and degrade the user experience.

name: use-domain-appropriate-types-for-monetary-values
content: When representing monetary values (e.g., discount amounts, prices), use a domain-appropriate type that includes both the amount and the currency (e.g., a `Money` type) rather than a bare number or string. This prevents currency ambiguity and makes the data model self-documenting.

name: declare-shared-types-in-module-not-pages
content: Declare shared or reusable types (e.g., domain models, form types) in the relevant module or shared types file rather than in page files or leaf components. When extending a type with additional fields (e.g., adding `stock` to `OrderItem`), prefer updating the existing type definition at its source rather than augmenting it inline at the usage site. Never import types from a pages folder into other modules.

name: avoid-misleading-navigation-on-failure
content: Do not navigate away (e.g., to a list or success page) after a mutation unless you have confirmed the operation succeeded (e.g., a valid ID or success flag was returned). Navigating on an ambiguous or missing result creates a false impression that the action completed successfully and can confuse users.

name: prefer-standard-disabled-prop-over-derived-logic
content: When a component needs to be disabled based on external state, expose a simple `disabled` boolean prop rather than accepting domain-specific state (e.g., a nullable condition type) and deriving the disabled state internally. This keeps the component API clean, generic, and decoupled from business logic.

name: prefer-existing-libraries-for-data-manipulation
content: Before implementing custom logic for common data manipulation tasks (e.g., date formatting, string parsing, math operations), check whether an already-imported library (e.g., date-fns, lodash) provides the functionality. Reusing existing library utilities reduces custom code, avoids bugs, and keeps the codebase consistent.

name: avoid-overlapping-callback-props
content: Avoid exposing multiple callback props on a component that serve overlapping or ambiguous purposes (e.g., both `onAddRule` and `onUpdateRule` called together in the same submit handler). Consolidate them into a single, clearly named callback or differentiate them with unambiguous semantics to prevent confusion and misuse.

name: use-structured-object-props-instead-of-primitive-fragmentation
content: When a component receives multiple related primitive props that together represent a single concept (e.g., `selectedChannelId` and `selectedChannelName`), consolidate them into a single structured object prop (e.g., `selected: { id: string; name: string }`). This reduces prop count, improves cohesion, and makes the component's intent clearer.

name: prefer-null-over-undefined-for-default-values
content: Prefer `null` over `undefined` as default values for optional or missing data, especially in form state and API payloads. Using `null` makes the absence of a value explicit and intentional, aligns with JSON semantics, and avoids ambiguity between 'not set' and 'not provided'.

name: colocate-domain-utilities-in-feature-module
content: Place domain-specific utility functions (e.g., discount formatters, price calculators) in the feature module they belong to (e.g., a discount formatter belongs in the discount module, not a common/utils folder). Only move utilities to a shared or common module if they are truly domain-agnostic and reused across multiple unrelated modules.

name: use-design-system-tokens-for-computed-css-values
content: Avoid inline computed CSS expressions (e.g., `calc(0.5rem - 2px)`) when the desired value can be expressed using an existing design system token or variable. Define reusable values in the tokens file and reference them by name to keep styles consistent and maintainable.

name: map-variant-props-to-distinct-visual-elements
content: When a component supports multiple variants (e.g., info, warning, danger), ensure that all visual elements — including icons, colors, and other indicators — change appropriately per variant rather than using a single static value for all. This ensures the component accurately communicates its intent for each variant.

name: keep-conditional-ui-elements-within-their-condition-scope
content: Ensure that UI elements (e.g., separators, labels, decorators) that are logically tied to a conditional block are placed inside that block rather than outside it. Rendering decorative or contextual elements unconditionally when their associated content is hidden adds visual noise and can confuse users.

name: use-form-library-for-multi-field-forms
content: When a form or interactive UI has more than one related input field (e.g., a comment and a rating value), prefer using a form management library (e.g., Formik, React Hook Form) over managing each field with separate useState calls. This reduces boilerplate, centralizes validation, and improves maintainability.

name: use-single-styling-approach-per-component
content: Avoid mixing multiple styling approaches (e.g., inline styles, CSS modules, and Panda CSS) within the same component. Pick a single, consistent styling method — preferring the project's designated system (e.g., Panda CSS) — to keep styles predictable, maintainable, and consistent with the rest of the codebase.

name: prefer-descriptive-boolean-variable-names
content: When deriving a boolean from a condition, assign it to a named variable with a descriptive name that clearly expresses its meaning (e.g., `isBatchRequest` instead of `isMultiple` or an inline expression). This improves readability and makes the intent explicit at the point of use.

name: avoid-platform-specific-shell-commands
content: Avoid committing platform-specific shell commands (e.g., Windows `xcopy`, `rmdir /s`) or scripts into shared configuration files. Use cross-platform alternatives (e.g., `rimraf`, `cpx`, or npm scripts that work on all OSes) to ensure CI pipelines and teammates on different operating systems are not broken.

name: remove-unused-files-before-merging
content: Remove unused or empty files (e.g., CSS modules, utility files, components) before merging a PR. If a component is no longer needed, delete it rather than leaving a TODO comment in place. Leftover files add noise to the codebase and can cause confusion about what is actually in use.

name: handle-all-supported-type-variants
content: When a component or function accepts a type discriminator (e.g., a `type` prop with a union of string literals), ensure all declared variants are handled. Unhandled cases should either be implemented or removed from the type definition to keep the contract accurate.

name: keep-component-layout-concerns-at-call-site
content: Keep layout-specific wrapper elements (e.g., `<td>`, fixed-width containers) out of reusable components. Instead, apply such wrappers at the call site where the component is used, so the component remains flexible and reusable across different layout contexts.

name: avoid-type-assertion-casting
content: Avoid using TypeScript `as` type assertions to cast values to a specific type, especially when the underlying data type is uncertain or comes from an external source (e.g., an API or query state). Instead, use proper type guards, runtime validation, or ensure the type is correctly inferred at the source. Type assertions bypass the type checker and can hide real type mismatches.

name: fix-typos-and-copy-errors-before-merging
content: Fix all typos and copy errors in user-facing strings, variable names, and code identifiers (e.g., `isNOtValid` → `isNotValid`) before merging a PR. Typos in UI text degrade the user experience and typos in code identifiers reduce readability and can mask bugs.

name: place-providers-at-app-root-level
content: Place shared context providers (e.g., TooltipProvider, QueryClientProvider) in the application's root providers file (e.g., `providers/index.tsx`) rather than nesting them inside individual components. This ensures the provider is available globally, avoids redundant instantiation, and keeps the component tree clean.

name: avoid-redundant-z-index-declarations
content: Avoid adding z-index values to multiple sibling or parent-child elements within the same stacking context when only one declaration is necessary. Review existing z-index usage before adding new ones to ensure they are not duplicated or conflicting.

name: use-action-oriented-button-labels
content: Use clear, action-oriented labels for call-to-action buttons (e.g., 'Load More', 'Save Changes', 'Submit') that accurately describe what will happen when the user clicks them. Avoid vague or non-descriptive labels that do not communicate the button's purpose.

name: keep-generated-files-in-sync-with-schema
content: Regenerate auto-generated files (e.g., GraphQL schema types, OpenAPI clients) whenever the underlying schema changes, such as when a mutation or type is added, modified, or removed. Stale generated files can introduce phantom types and mislead developers about what the API actually supports.

name: fetch-context-data-server-side
content: Fetch context-derived data (e.g., organization ID, user session) on the server side rather than inside client components via hooks. This keeps client components lean, avoids redundant client-side requests, and respects the server/client boundary.

name: prefer-url-query-params-for-persistent-ui-state
content: When UI state (e.g., selected date range, active tab, filter values) should survive a page refresh or be shareable via URL, store it as a query parameter rather than in local component state. This improves UX by preserving context across refreshes and enabling deep-linking.

name: maintain-consistent-domain-terminology
content: Use consistent domain terminology across the codebase (e.g., always use 'Stock' instead of mixing 'Stock' and 'Inventory'). Inconsistent wording in variable names, hooks, and UI text creates confusion; align naming with the agreed-upon domain language and update legacy references when discovered.

name: handle-error-states-consistently-across-components
content: When adding error state handling to a data-fetching component (e.g., displaying a 'Something went wrong' message), ensure that all similar components in the same feature or module handle errors consistently. Audit related components when introducing error handling in one to avoid a patchwork of inconsistent UX.

name: remove-unnecessary-inline-styles
content: Remove CSS property declarations or style overrides that are no longer needed after a refactor or component update (e.g., a font-weight override that becomes redundant after a layout change). Unused style rules add noise and can conflict with design system defaults.

name: avoid-empty-or-placeholder-components
content: Do not commit components or render functions that return empty or meaningless markup (e.g., an empty `<Stack></Stack>`). Either implement the component's intended content or remove it entirely until it is ready. Placeholder components add noise and can mislead readers about the codebase's completeness.

name: justify-polling-with-explicit-rationale
content: Do not add `pollInterval` or other polling behavior to a query unless there is a clear, documented reason for it (e.g., real-time status updates on a background job). If polling is not needed, remove it to avoid unnecessary network traffic and unintended side effects.

name: derive-currency-from-data-not-hardcoded
content: Do not hardcode currency values (e.g., always displaying 'EUR') when the data may represent multiple currencies. Derive the currency from the query response or domain data so that the UI accurately reflects the actual currency for each record.

name: consolidate-queries-when-possible
content: Avoid issuing multiple separate queries in a single component when the data can be fetched in one request. Consolidate queries where possible to reduce network overhead, simplify component logic, and avoid inconsistent loading states.

name: validate-business-rules-on-both-client-and-server
content: When implementing business logic validation (e.g., filtering order items by channel, enforcing constraints), ensure the validation is enforced on both the client and the server. Client-side-only validation can be bypassed and should always be backed by equivalent server-side checks.

name: avoid-hardcoded-validation-limits-without-justification
content: Avoid introducing or changing hardcoded validation limits (e.g., max file count, max upload size) without a clear, documented reason. If a constraint does not align with the actual requirement (e.g., restricting file count when only file size should be restricted), remove or correct it to match the intended behavior.

name: use-form-validation-for-field-level-rules
content: Enforce field-level constraints (e.g., required fields, format checks) through the form validation schema (e.g., Yup, Zod) rather than as ad-hoc logic inside event handlers or components. Centralizing validation in the schema keeps rules consistent, reusable, and easier to maintain.

name: use-component-built-in-props-before-wrapping
content: Before wrapping a component with another element or adding layout wrappers inside it (e.g., wrapping icon + text in an HStack inside a Button), check whether the component already supports the needed behavior via a built-in prop (e.g., a `prefix` prop on Button for leading icons, an `href` prop on Menu.Item for navigation). Using built-in props reduces unnecessary DOM nesting and keeps the code simpler.

name: use-slot-pattern-for-flexible-component-composition
content: When a component needs to render a customizable trigger or wrapper element (e.g., a button that opens a dialog), prefer using a Slot pattern (e.g., `<Slot>`) to delegate rendering to the caller rather than hardcoding a specific trigger element. This keeps the component flexible and composable without coupling it to a specific UI element.

name: ensure-select-inputs-support-deselection
content: When implementing a select or picker component (e.g., CategorySelect, ChannelSelect), ensure users can deselect or clear the current selection when the field is optional. Omitting a deselect option can trap users with an unwanted value and degrade the form experience.

name: verify-client-side-filtering-does-not-break-pagination
content: When applying client-side filters or transformations (e.g., filtering fetched results in a useMemo), verify that doing so does not interfere with pagination or infinite scroll logic. Client-side filtering on paginated data can cause incomplete result sets and incorrect 'load more' behavior; prefer server-side filtering where pagination is involved.

name: use-consistent-callback-prop-naming
content: Name callback props consistently, descriptively, and at the appropriate level of specificity across similar components (e.g., prefer `onChange` and `onRemove` over verbose domain-specific names like `onItemQuantityChange` and `onItemRemove` when the component's context already implies the domain). Consistent, well-scoped naming makes the component API self-documenting and reduces verbosity.

name: colocate-tightly-coupled-components
content: Place components that are tightly coupled in terms of functionality or context (e.g., a step tracker and its action buttons used exclusively within a modal) in the same directory. The guiding principle is: if a component cannot be meaningfully used without another, they should be co-located. Only separate them if the component makes sense as a standalone, reusable unit outside that context.

name: avoid-useeffect-for-validation
content: Do not use useEffect to perform validation logic (e.g., validating a URL token on page load) that will naturally be enforced when the user submits or triggers the relevant action. Prefer letting the server-side action or form submission handler enforce such validation, keeping components simpler and side-effect-free.

name: prefer-css-text-overflow-for-truncation
content: When truncating long text in UI components, prefer native CSS text overflow techniques (e.g., `text-overflow: ellipsis` with `overflow: hidden` and `white-space: nowrap`) over JavaScript-based character limiting or manual substring logic. CSS-based truncation is more performant, responsive, and requires less custom code.

name: scope-shared-component-changes-carefully
content: When modifying a shared or design system component (e.g., changing a dimension, adding a visual indicator, or altering layout), consider the impact on all consumers of that component. If the change should only apply to a specific use case, override it at the call site rather than modifying the shared component, and verify that universal changes are intentional and reviewed broadly.

name: extract-inline-types-to-named-types
content: When an inline type literal (e.g., `{ id: string; name: string }`) is used in more than one place or is complex enough to warrant a name, extract it into a named type or interface above the component. This improves readability, enables reuse, and makes refactoring easier.

name: reuse-existing-validation-schemas
content: When an existing validation schema (e.g., `addressValidationSchema`) already covers the required fields, reuse it rather than duplicating or writing a custom schema inline. Centralizing validation schemas reduces duplication and ensures consistent validation rules across the codebase.

name: guard-no-op-mutations-with-preconditions
content: Before triggering a mutation or update operation, check whether the new value actually differs from the existing state (e.g., skip updating avatar if `isMedia(values.avatar)` already holds). Skipping no-op mutations reduces unnecessary network requests and avoids unintended side effects.

name: avoid-hardcoded-boolean-field-values
content: Avoid hardcoding boolean field values (e.g., `managedInventory: true`) in form submissions or mutation payloads unless the value is truly invariant and intentional. If the value should always be true, document why; otherwise expose it as a configurable field to avoid silent behavioral constraints.

name: throw-on-missing-required-context
content: When a value (e.g., organization ID, session data) is required for a page or component to function correctly, throw an explicit error or redirect when it is undefined rather than silently proceeding with an undefined value. This surfaces misconfiguration early and prevents subtle bugs caused by missing context.

name: prefer-standard-yup-validation-over-custom-test
content: When writing Yup validation schemas, prefer using built-in Yup validators (e.g., `Yup.string().required()`, `Yup.object()`) over custom `.test()` functions when the built-in API already covers the requirement. Reserve `.test()` for validations that genuinely cannot be expressed with standard Yup methods.

name: remove-unused-state-variables
content: Remove state variables (e.g., `setVariantLoading`) that are declared but never meaningfully used in a component. Unused state adds confusion, increases re-renders, and should be deleted before merging.

name: use-subtle-visual-hierarchy-for-secondary-content
content: Use subtle visual styling (e.g., muted colors, smaller font sizes, lighter weights) for secondary or descriptive UI elements such as action descriptions or metadata, so they do not compete visually with primary content. This improves readability and maintains a clear visual hierarchy.

name: map-variant-types-to-dedicated-components
content: When rendering different UI elements based on a type discriminator (e.g., activity type, notification kind), prefer creating a dedicated component per variant and mapping the type to its component, rather than using large if/else chains or switch statements inline. This improves readability and makes it easy to add or modify individual variants independently.

name: avoid-duplicate-shared-components
content: Before creating a new component (e.g., a step circle, timeline node, or activity indicator), check whether an equivalent component already exists elsewhere in the codebase (e.g., in a drawer or timeline module). Reuse the existing component rather than creating a near-duplicate to keep the codebase consistent and reduce maintenance burden.

name: separate-skeleton-from-content-components
content: When a component supports both a skeleton/loading state and a content state via a boolean prop (e.g., `skeleton?: boolean`), split them into two distinct components instead. This keeps each component focused on a single responsibility and avoids conditional branching that complicates the component's logic and markup.

name: use-correct-prop-types-to-avoid-casting
content: Use accurate and precise TypeScript types on component props so that type casting (e.g., `as SomeType`) is not needed at the usage site. If you find yourself casting a prop value, revisit the prop's type definition and correct it at the source rather than patching it with assertions downstream.

name: avoid-parallel-duplicate-state-structures
content: When a form or component manages two parallel data structures that represent the same concept (e.g., two address objects that must be kept in sync), consolidate them into a single source of truth where possible. Maintaining duplicate state increases complexity and makes updates error-prone.

name: prefer-appropriate-filter-operators
content: When filtering or searching data via an API, use the most semantically appropriate filter operator for the use case (e.g., prefer a `Search` operator over an equality `Eq` operator for user-facing search inputs). Using the correct operator improves search UX and avoids returning overly restrictive results.

name: ensure-selected-entities-have-required-identifiers
content: When a user selects an entity (e.g., an address, a product, a channel) from a list or picker, ensure the selected value always includes its unique identifier (e.g., `id`). Do not make `id` optional on a selected entity type, as downstream operations (e.g., mutations, navigation) depend on it being defined.

name: fetch-only-required-fields-in-queries
content: When writing GraphQL queries, only select the fields that are actually needed for the UI or logic consuming the query. Fetching unused fields (e.g., `address` when only `name` and `avatar` are needed) increases payload size and obscures intent; trim queries to the minimum required set.

name: avoid-committing-incomplete-or-commented-out-features
content: Do not commit commented-out code or incomplete features intended to be 'added later' as inline comments in production files. If a feature is not ready, track it via a proper issue or feature branch rather than leaving it as commented-out code in the main codebase.

name: use-kebab-case-for-url-query-params
content: Always use kebab-case for URL query parameter names (e.g., `order-statuses` instead of `orderStatuses`). Since query parameters appear in the URL, camelCase names are unconventional and can cause confusion; kebab-case is the standard convention for URL-safe identifiers.

name: implement-graceful-display-name-fallbacks
content: When displaying a user's name, always implement a graceful fallback chain (e.g., show `firstname lastname` if available, otherwise fall back to `email`). Avoid removing fallback logic that was intentionally providing a safer display path, as users may have incomplete profile data.
